summary: American film director (1894–1973), a foundational figure of Hollywood and the Western genre. Known for Westerns starring John Wayne: Stagecoach (1939, first Monument Valley film), The Searchers (1956), The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (1962), The Quiet Man (1952). Also directed The Grapes of Wrath (1940) with cinematographer Gregg Toland. Andrew Sarris placed him in the auteur theory 'pantheon.' Famous line: 'When the legend becomes fact, print the legend.'

links.text: The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance — Wikipedia

works.name: The Searchers (1956)
works.description: John Wayne plays Ethan Edwards, who obsessively searches for his niece Debbie after she is captured by the Comanche (led by Scar). Jeffrey Hunter plays his companion Martin Pawley. Ethan often says 'That'll be the day' to Martin. The film famously begins and ends with shots of Ethan framed in a cabin doorway — he looks in at his brother's old home, then turns and walks away. Debbie is at one point nearly shot by Ethan when she says she wants to remain with the Comanche. A fistfight involving 'somebody's fiddle' delays Laurie's wedding. The film was shot in Monument Valley. It heavily influenced Lawrence of Arabia's mirage-at-a-well sequence. Zacharias Kunuk made an Inuit adaptation. Considered Ford's masterpiece.

clues.clue: Ethan Edwards (John Wayne) seeks niece Debbie, captured by the Comanche; Ethan says 'That'll be the day' to companion Martin Pawley (Jeffrey Hunter)
examples: This film's protagonist often remarks 'That'll be the day' to his companion Martin Pawley, played by a young Jeffrey Hunter.
In one of this man's films, Ethan Edwards, tries to shoot his daughter when she says she wants to join her kidnappers.

clues.clue: Famous opening and closing: Ethan framed in a cabin doorway; he turns and walks away at the end — a major doorway motif runs throughout Ford's films
examples: This man's most famous film begins and ends with shots of a man framed by a cabin door.
The iconic last shot of one of this director's films shows Ethan Edwards look into the door of his brother's old house, but then turn and walk away.

works.name: The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (1962)
works.description: James Stewart plays Ranse Stoddard, a senator who returns to the town of Shinbone for the funeral of his old friend Tom Doniphon (John Wayne). In flashback, it emerges that Tom (not Ranse) actually killed Liberty Valance, a whip-cracking villain. Tom addresses Ranse as 'pilgrim.' A black character named Pompey recites the Declaration of Independence in front of a portrait of Lincoln. In the Gold Room, a printing press is destroyed by Liberty Valance. At the end, when the newspaper editor learns the truth, he burns his notes and says: 'When the legend becomes fact, print the legend.' A still photograph from the 1880s party features Jack (Pompey) shooting a can of paint that explodes over Ranse. Stoddard returns to tell the conductor 'nothing's too good for the man who shot Liberty Valance.'

clues.clue: 'When the legend becomes fact, print the legend' — the newspaper editor burns his notes after discovering Tom Doniphon actually killed Liberty Valance
examples: In a film by this director, a character lampoons the myths around American history when he says 'when the legend becomes fact, print the legend'.
A newspaper editor says 'When the legend becomes fact, print the legend' to explain his decision not to publicize the fact that it was Tom, not Ranse, who performed this action.

clues.clue: Tom Doniphon (John Wayne rancher, calls Stewart's character 'pilgrim') is the true killer; Ranse Stoddard (Jimmy Stewart) becomes senator; begins with Ranse returning for Tom's funeral
examples: Tom Doniphon, a rancher who addresses Jimmy Stewart's character as 'pilgrim,' kills the title outlaw in a film by this director of The Quiet Man.
This film begins with Jimmy Stewart's character Ranse Stoddard coming back to a town to attend the funeral of his friend Tom Doniphon, who perpetrated the title act.

clues.clue: Black character Pompey recites the Declaration of Independence in front of a Lincoln portrait; Liberty Valance destroys a printing press; title character whip-cracking
examples: In one film by this director, a black character named Pompey recites the Declaration of Independence while standing in front of a portrait of Lincoln.
In that film by this director, a printing press is destroyed by a villain who also assaults the lawyer Rance Stoddard with his trademark whip.

works.name: Stagecoach (1939)
works.description: John Ford's first film shot in Monument Valley and first collaboration with John Wayne. Apaches chase the title vehicle. The shot briefly goes out of focus as it zooms in to introduce a man who twirls a rifle in one hand and holds a saddle in the other (Wayne's introduction). A Southern gambler aims his last bullet at the woman beside him just before a bugle call sounds in the distance. In a dinner scene, all characters but one choose to sit apart from a certain woman. The negative is lost; extant releases use a positive print discovered by Peter Bogdanovich in Wayne's garage. The film has a major doorway motif like The Searchers.

clues.clue: First Ford-Wayne collaboration; first Monument Valley film; Apaches chase the title vehicle; Wayne's character introduced with an out-of-focus zoom as he twirls a rifle
examples: This was the first of its director's films to shoot in Monument Valley.
For 10 points, in what first collaboration between John Ford and John Wayne do Apaches chase the title vehicle?

clues.clue: The film's negative is lost; extant releases use a positive print found by Peter Bogdanovich in John Wayne's garage
examples: Since the negative is lost, this film's rather grainy extant releases use a positive print discovered by Peter Bogdanovich in the star's garage.

works.name: The Quiet Man (1952)
works.description: John Wayne plays Sean Thornton, an Irish-American boxer who returns to Ireland after accidentally killing an opponent. He falls in love with a local woman. The climax is a ridiculously long fistfight between Sean Thornton and Will Danaher (Victor McLaglen) that begins in the countryside and ends miles away in Cohan's Bar. A fistfight in this film inspired a similar long fight in John Carpenter's They Live.

clues.clue: Sean Thornton (John Wayne), former boxer who accidentally killed an opponent, returns to Ireland; climax is a long fistfight with Will Danaher (Victor McLaglen) across the countryside
examples: In another of this man's films, Sean Thornton returns to his home country of Ireland and hides his past life as a boxer who accidentally killed an opponent.
A ridiculously long one of these events involving Will Danaher and Sean Thornton, played by Victor McLaglen and John Wayne, is the climax of The Quiet Man.

works.name: The Grapes of Wrath (1940) and Cinematographer Gregg Toland
works.description: Ford partnered with cinematographer Gregg Toland for The Long Voyage Home (1940, adaptation of three Eugene O'Neill plays) and The Grapes of Wrath (1940). The Grapes of Wrath was a massive adaptation of the Steinbeck novel. Toland's work on these Ford films helped develop deep focus cinematography — keeping foreground, middle ground, and background all clearly visible — before he applied it to Citizen Kane with Orson Welles. Andre Bazin admired Toland's deep focus. Ford also shot My Darling Clementine (with Henry Fonda as Wyatt Earp) in Monument Valley.

clues.clue: Ford worked with deep-focus cinematographer Gregg Toland on The Long Voyage Home and The Grapes of Wrath; Toland then brought deep focus to Citizen Kane with Welles
examples: Toland refined his deep focus craft on this director's film of The Grapes of Wrath.
In 1940, he partnered with Toland for The Long Voyage Home, an adaptation of three Eugene O'Neill plays, and an adaptation of The Grapes of Wrath.

works.name: Style, Legacy, and Monument Valley
works.description: John Ford described his style as 'invisible technique,' though he later refused to discuss his work. He frequently used shots of trains, boats, or wagons to begin and end films. Monument Valley (on the Arizona-Utah border) served as the backdrop for Stagecoach, The Searchers, My Darling Clementine, and many other Ford Westerns. In an interview at Monument Valley, Ford angrily cut off Peter Bogdanovich's question about 'the point' of one of his films. Andrew Sarris placed Ford in the auteur theory 'pantheon' in his book American Cinema alongside Orson Welles and twelve other directors. The Cahiers du Cinema editors wrote an analysis of Ford's Young Mr. Lincoln. Ford's career included a transcontinental railroad epic depicting Irish, Italian, Chinese immigrants and African-Americans. An Austrian immigrant dancing in a music hall window is a characteristic early scene. Ford's fistfights are a recurring motif across many films (Donovan's Reef, The Searchers, The Quiet Man).

clues.clue: Ford called his style 'invisible technique'; frequently used shots of trains, boats, and wagons to open/close films
examples: Though he later refused to talk about his work at all, this director used the term 'invisible technique' to describe his style.
This director frequently used shots of trains, boats, or wagons to begin and end films like The Sun Shines Bright and Doctor Bull.

clues.clue: Monument Valley (Arizona/Utah) is the backdrop for Stagecoach, The Searchers, My Darling Clementine, and other Ford Westerns; Ford angrily cut off Bogdanovich's interview there
examples: My Darling Clementine is among the many films this director shot in Monument Valley.
For 10 points, name this iconic Arizona and Utah location where portions of Stagecoach, The Searchers, and other films of John Ford were shot.

clues.clue: Andrew Sarris placed Ford at the top of his auteur theory 'pantheon' in American Cinema alongside Orson Welles; Cahiers du Cinema analyzed Young Mr. Lincoln
examples: A book by this critic that establishes a 'pantheon' consisting of Orson Welles, John Ford, and twelve other directors popularized the English name of the idea that the director is the sole creative force behind any particular film.
To demonstrate auteur theory, the editors of Cahiers du Cinema wrote an analysis of this director's film Young Mr. Lincoln.

comprehensive_summary:
John Ford (1894–1973) was a foundational American film director, known primarily for Westerns starring John Wayne. His films are characterized by Monument Valley (Arizona/Utah) landscapes, a doorway motif, recurring fistfights, and what he called 'invisible technique.' He frequently used shots of trains, boats, or wagons to begin and end films.

The Searchers (1956) is often considered his masterpiece: John Wayne plays Ethan Edwards, who obsessively searches for his niece Debbie (captured by the Comanche leader Scar). Ethan often says 'That'll be the day' to companion Martin Pawley (Jeffrey Hunter). The film famously begins and ends with shots of Ethan framed in a cabin doorway. The Searchers heavily influenced Lawrence of Arabia's mirage-at-a-well sequence; Zacharias Kunuk later made an Inuit adaptation.

The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (1962) stars James Stewart as senator Ranse Stoddard and John Wayne as rancher Tom Doniphon (who calls Ranse 'pilgrim'). Tom actually killed Liberty Valance, but Ranse got the credit. A black character named Pompey recites the Declaration of Independence before a Lincoln portrait. A printing press is destroyed by Liberty Valance. The climax's newspaper editor, learning the truth, burns his notes and says: 'When the legend becomes fact, print the legend.'

Stagecoach (1939) was Ford's first Monument Valley film and first Wayne collaboration; Apaches chase the title vehicle; the film's negative is lost, and extant copies come from a positive print found by Peter Bogdanovich in Wayne's garage. The Quiet Man (1952) features Wayne as Sean Thornton, a former boxer returning to Ireland, with a climactic long fistfight against Will Danaher (Victor McLaglen).

For The Grapes of Wrath (1940) and The Long Voyage Home (1940), Ford worked with cinematographer Gregg Toland, who pioneered deep focus before bringing it to Citizen Kane with Orson Welles. My Darling Clementine featured Henry Fonda as Wyatt Earp. Ford appears in Andrew Sarris's auteur theory 'pantheon' in American Cinema; Cahiers du Cinema analyzed Young Mr. Lincoln.
recursive_suggestions: John Wayne — Ford's primary actor; appeared in Stagecoach, The Searchers, The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance, The Quiet Man (Fine Arts)
Gregg Toland — cinematographer on The Grapes of Wrath and Citizen Kane; pioneered deep focus (Fine Arts)
The Searchers — highest-frequency Ford answerline; often treated as its own topic (Fine Arts)

Film: 'When the legend becomes fact, print the legend'; Tom Doniphon (John Wayne, calls Stewart's character 'pilgrim') is the true killer; senator Ranse Stoddard (Jimmy Stewart) begins by returning for Tom's funeral; black character Pompey recites the Declaration of Independence before a Lincoln portrait
cards.back: The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (John Ford, 1962)
cards.work: The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (1962)

cards.front: Film: Ford's first Monument Valley film and first Wayne collaboration; Apaches chase the title vehicle; Wayne's introduction — shot goes out of focus as he twirls a rifle; film's negative is lost, extant releases use positive print found by Bogdanovich in Wayne's garage

Film: Sean Thornton (John Wayne), Irish-American boxer who accidentally killed an opponent, returns to Ireland; climax is a ridiculously long fistfight with Will Danaher (Victor McLaglen) from countryside to Cohan's Bar; inspired a long fight in John Carpenter's They Live

Director: called his style 'invisible technique'; Monument Valley backdrop for Stagecoach, The Searchers, My Darling Clementine; worked with Gregg Toland (deep focus) on The Grapes of Wrath; Andrew Sarris placed him in auteur theory 'pantheon'; Cahiers du Cinema analyzed his Young Mr. Lincoln
